nodes.text: I didn't know what to expect when I signed. 'Classified posting. Accept or decline.' No briefing, no details — just a signature line and a car waiting outside. I certainly didn't expect to walk into the pages of a science-fiction comic. Interstellar travel through a glowing ring. Well. That's new. ... Since Cassy passed, I could use the work. Maybe the distance more.

options.text: Go down.

nodes.text: Four hundred meters down, the ring hangs in its cradle like a held breath. As of this morning it's mine — along with a budget that says the Consortium expects nothing. Dr. Okafor waits with an address ledger. Captain Mercer waits with a weapons manifest. Everyone down here is waiting on me. First day. No pressure.

options.text: Start with the science. Okafor first.
Start with the threats. Mercer first.

nodes.text: Okafor decoded the address notation in six weeks. My predecessor's notes call her indispensable and exhausting. I can work with both. She hands me the brief she prepared before I asked for one: fifty-one addresses, one dialed. Recon One went through to Address 04 eleven days ago. Two check-ins — breathable air, terraced fields, a village on a white road — then nine days of nothing. 'The gate holds,' she says. 'That's what frightens me.'

options.text: Now the threats. Mercer.

nodes.text: Mercer. I read the file — a decade of deployments, some so classified even I wasn't cleared to know we'd been there. By the book: fast, efficient, no loose ends. Good. I'll need the book today. His requisitions were signed before I sat down. 'Recon One. Four people, two check-ins, nine days of silence. Somebody stopped them checking in. Plan for that, and you get to be wrong in the good direction.'

options.text: Now the science. Okafor.

nodes.text: Mercer finds me before I send for him. I read the file — a decade of deployments, some so classified even I wasn't cleared to know we'd been there. By the book: fast, efficient, no loose ends. Good. I'll need the book today. He hears Okafor's brief in silence, then: 'Somebody stopped them checking in. Plan for that, and you get to be wrong in the good direction.'

options.text: To the dialing floor.

nodes.text: Okafor decoded the address notation in six weeks. My predecessor's notes call her indispensable and exhausting. I can work with both. She confirms Mercer's count without softening it: fifty-one addresses, one dialed, Recon One nine days dark on Address 04. 'The gate holds. The address holds. Whatever stopped them checking in, Director — it isn't the physics.'

options.text: To the dialing floor.

nodes.text: We can dial within the hour. Whatever is on the other side has had Recon One for nine days.

options.text: Assemble the team. We bring them home.
Assemble the team — but at the first sign of trouble, they come back.

nodes.text: The gate opens on a green valley under two suns — terraced fields, a road of packed white stone, cart ruts worn deep. Recon One's marker beacon lies ten meters out, snapped clean. No bodies. No shell casings. Up-valley, a bell rings once and stops, like a mistake.

options.text: Follow the road up-valley.

nodes.text: They come out of the field rows at a run — a dozen villagers, human as you are, sun-worn and urgent. Not one of them makes a sound. They pull at your arms and packs, herding you off the road toward the treeline, fingers pressed to lips. The oldest points down the road, then flattens his hand toward the earth: down. Hide. Now.

options.text: Trust them. Get off the road.
Break free. You don't surrender the road to strangers.

nodes.text: From the treeline you feel it before you see it: a tread that reaches you through the ground. Tenders. Insect-forms the size of freight cars, walking in single file, laden with grain, cloth, bound crates — tribute. They pass without one glance at the world around them, down the white road, to the gate. Through it. The villagers do not watch them. They watch you, willing you silent.

options.text: Stay down until the last one is gone.

nodes.text: The last porter passes and the villagers rise, herding you along the ditch toward the mill path — a hushed, half-running file. Then a boy, not a child, not yet a man, catches his foot in a snare-furrow and goes down hard, staves clattering. Down the road, two flanker Tenders — man-height, quick, heads ticking — swing back toward the sound. Mercer is already running the wrong way.

options.text: Get him behind cover. Hide.
No time to hide. Grab the boy and run for it.

nodes.text: Mercer pulls the boy behind a road-stone and holds him still. Steps. A dry snare-rattle, very close. He rotates them around the stone as the Tenders pass — always the stone between his back and the sweep of their eyeless heads — until the rattle thins away down the white road. In the field rows nobody breathes. Then the boy's father is there, pressing his forehead to his son's.

options.text: Let the villagers lead you clear.

nodes.text: By the treeline the elder draws glyphs in the dirt — slowly, stroke by stroke, so Okafor can copy every one. An address. He crosses his wrists: the bound. Four fingers: four like you, taken through the gate, alive when they passed. The place the taken are brought. A woman says its name aloud, the first word any of them has risked all day: Veyra.

options.text: Go home with the address.

nodes.text: Three strides and the flankers have the angle. Mercer throws the boy down, turns, and puts the first Tender on its back with a burst. The second is nearly on him when Okafor drops it — two rounds, no wasted motion, the range instructor's star pupil. The rattle stops. The whole valley has heard the shots.

options.text: Cover the villagers.

nodes.text: The father hauls the boy up and presses his forehead to his son's — once, hard. Then the villagers are running, not for the trees: for the gate. An old woman spins the dial-stones through an address Okafor memorizes on instinct; the ring opens and the field families pour through. The gate closes. Silence. They fled to the place the taken are brought — to their god's own door.

options.text: Note the address. Go home.

nodes.text: You shove clear and a quarterstaff cracks against Mercer's rifle — then it is a brawl in the treeline, silent and desperate, the villagers fighting like people who cannot afford to be heard. It ends wrong. A young villager is down, neck twisted against a root, not moving. That is when the ground begins to shake.

options.text: Freeze.

nodes.text: Every hand stops — theirs and yours. Tenders, insect-forms the size of freight cars, file down the white road under tribute loads, and every living thing in the valley holds still as stone until they pass — to the gate, and through it. Okafor checks the fallen villager for a pulse and finds none. Finds, instead, a hide cord at his throat, burned with gate glyphs: an address. The kind a whole valley wears so its dead can be spoken for where the taken are brought.

options.text: Pocket the cord. Withdraw before the others regroup.

nodes.text: The villagers return for their dead in silence, and the silence is a verdict. Whatever the cord means, wherever the taken go, someone in this valley knows — and no one here will ever tell you now. The glyphs on a dead boy's cord are the only lead you are leaving with.

options.text: Go home.

effects.text: Address 04: we hid with the villagers and they gave us the address where the taken are brought — Veyra. Four offworlders went through the gate alive. The valley trusts us with its dead.

outcomes.label: The address, read off the dial

effects.text: Address 04: two Tenders dead and the shots heard valley-wide. The field families fled through the gate to their god — Okafor holds the address they dialed. The taken went the same way.
Address 04: a villager is dead by our hands. His cord carries gate glyphs — the address where this valley's taken and dead are brought. The valley will remember us.

nodes.text: Karsu: a watermill village of stone and moss where the valley narrows, straddling the tribute road. Smoke from the chimneys. Eyes at the shutters. They knew you were coming before you crossed the bridge.

options.text: Approach the bridge.
Approach the bridge.
Approach the bridge.

nodes.text: The boy from the road meets you at the bridge, bruised and grinning, and all Karsu has heard the story by now: the offworlder who held him behind a stone and turned with the Tenders' own walk. Odel, the miller, opens the ledger-house door herself. 'Karsu pays what it owes. Come in.'

options.text: Go in.

nodes.text: By the mill fire they speak of the Luminous One the way farmers speak of weather: without doubt. He hears every prayer said on the tribute road — names spoken at dusk are answered by morning. He walks where he is worshipped. He has kept the valley from famine for nine generations, and the price is the tribute, and sometimes the price is people. They are not lying. You can tell.

options.text: Ask about the taken.

nodes.text: The ledger is a mill-book, flour-dusted, older than any hand in the room: every soul the god has taken, written down so someone remembers. Four entries in fresh ink bear an offworlder glyph. Odel touches each in turn — alive. Karsu's porters crossed with the last grain-tithe and saw them: pent below the Penitence, the prison the holy city keeps on Veyra, the world behind the gate.

options.text: Ask how grain — and porters — cross to Veyra.

nodes.speaker: Odel
nodes.text: She banks the fire before she answers, as if the name could hear. 'When the Graced last came down for a taking, the First Blade himself led them. Seryn Vael. He stood at the road shrine and drank his Portion — light, standing in a cup, and he drank it like water. Pray the Door is not his to keep on the day you cross.'

options.text: And the crossing?

nodes.speaker: Odel
nodes.text: The grain-tithe crosses twice a season: carts to the valley gate, porters through it, straight into the temple stores on the far side. Porters need papers, and papers can be arranged for those Karsu trusts. The spire on the pass road sings each train through — the Door opens for traffic it expects, in and out again. The next tithe leaves soon.

options.text: Take the porters' road. Leave Karsu your spare field medicine in thanks.
Refuse the favor — you'll cross as pilgrims, on your own terms.

nodes.text: A cart half-bars the bridge. The boy Mercer saved is not here — his family fled through the gate with the rest, and Karsu keeps their houses swept for a return nobody promises. Odel, the miller, comes out alone. 'You saved one of ours and cost us the field families. After the shots, the Graced walked this valley for a day — the First Blade's own, hunting. Both things are true. Say what you want.'

options.text: Ask about the taken.

nodes.speaker: Odel
nodes.text: She brings the mill-book out to the bridge rather than open the door: every taken soul, written so someone remembers. Four fresh entries wear an offworlder glyph — alive, the porters say, pent below the Penitence on Veyra, behind the gate. 'The grain-tithe crosses soon, and the pass spire sings it through the Door. Papers can be had — for those who pay for the risk they carry with them.'

options.text: Pay for papers and a place in the tithe train.
Keep your materials. Cross as pilgrims, on your own terms.

nodes.text: A cart bars the bridge. Behind it stands all of Karsu, silent the way the valley goes silent for the Tenders. They know what happened in the trees. Odel, the miller, comes forward alone and speaks across the barrier, because their law demands the taken be spoken for — even to you.

options.text: Listen.

nodes.speaker: Odel
nodes.text: 'The Luminous One keeps the valley fed; the price is tribute, and sometimes people — written in the ledger, so someone remembers. Your four are alive, pent below the Penitence, on Veyra, behind the gate. When the pass spire sings, the tithe crosses. You will not be on it. And pray the First Blade is not at the Door when you find your own way through.' The shutters close.

options.text: Cross as pilgrims, then — cold, heavy, alone.

effects.text: Recon One is alive below the Penitence on Veyra. Karsu will pass us through with the grain-tithe — first, the pass spires whose call sings each train through the Door, and back out again.
Recon One is alive below the Penitence on Veyra. No place in the tithe train — we cross as pilgrims, and the spires on the pass road come first: their call is what opens the Door outward.

nodes.text: The team comes back through the gate ahead of the Tenders' reach, bruised and short of answers. The spires still sing on the pass road — the tribute call goes on with or without you. By morning Mercer has re-planned the approach.

options.text: Go again.

effects.text: The pass spires are still in temple hands. We go again.

nodes.text: Veyra. The Door stands open in its wardhouse on the pilgrim terrace, and it opens one way: the wardens dial no one out without the temple's word. Above the terrace the holy city climbs, white stone and bell-towers, to the ringed precinct of the Penitence — where four names in fresh ink are waiting. The crossing in is free. It is the door home that is shut.

options.text: Cross with Karsu's tithe train — porters' papers, grain on your shoulders, expected traffic.
Cross as pilgrims — two more dusty travelers among the many the Door receives.

nodes.text: Faces on the terrace.

options.text: Among the pilgrims: the boy from the road, and his father, come up with the valley's tithe. The father finds Mercer's eye and folds both hands flat — the valley's thanks, given where the god can see it. The villagers use the network too. They always have.

By the wardhouse, in penitent gray: the boy from the road, and his father — the field families that fled, come to beg pardon for the dead Tenders. The father presses his forehead to his son's, then looks at you: fear and debt in the same face. The villagers use the network too.

By the wardhouse, in penitent gray: a face from the treeline — the dead boy's father, come to speak his son's name where the taken and the dead are brought. He looks at you, and through you, and turns away. He knows exactly who you are. The villagers use the network too.

nodes.text: A day to work before the plan has to be a plan. Time enough to learn something, if you're careful about who's listening.

options.text: Listen among the pilgrims.
Shadow the temple patrols.
Okafor: tap the temple relay traffic.
We've learned enough. Move to the plan.

nodes.text: Words drift between processions: 'Oru walks where he is worshipped.' Doctrine, not proof — but it's the first crack in how they explain him.

options.text: Continue.

nodes.text: Guard rotations are looser than they look — predictable, if you watch long enough.

options.text: Continue.

nodes.speaker: Dr. A. Okafor
nodes.text: Okafor taps the relay traffic and goes quiet for a long moment. 'That's encrypted,' she finally says. 'Gods don't need encryption.'

options.text: Continue.

nodes.text: Three ways in, and none of them easy.

options.text: Guard uniforms — walk in as temple staff.
Provision workers — go in with the grain carts.
Open assault — go in loud.

nodes.text: The barracks courtyard empties out before shift change.

options.text: Knock out a lone guard and take his colors.
Find another way: lift a set from the bath-house during vigil hour.

nodes.text: He's out cold and will stay that way a while. What now?

options.text: Hide him in the cistern.
Leave him. Move fast.

nodes.text: Broker Dessik will get you work passes — for a price. His son Ilo is condemned as a smuggler, held somewhere in the Penitence. 'Bring him out,' Dessik says, 'and the carts are yours.'

options.text: Swear it.
Refuse. Find another way in.

nodes.text: The plan carries you to the temple gate. Whatever happens next, it happens fast.

options.text: Approach in guard colors.
Approach with the grain carts.
Hit the gate head-on.

nodes.text: Guard colors, temple gate, morning shift change.

options.text: Move for the checkpoint.
Move for the checkpoint.
Move for the checkpoint.

nodes.text: A sergeant squints at your face. He swears he's seen the missing guard's watch on your wrist.

options.text: Talk him down.
Take the checkpoint quietly.
Push past before he thinks it through.

nodes.text: The inner gate wants a rank-seal your borrowed colors don't have.

options.text: Bluff as new transfers.
Bluff as new transfers.
Slip through the ossuary passage instead.
Push past before they think it through.

nodes.text: Cell block. Rows of doors — and behind one, four faces gone gaunt. Barros is at the bars before the light finds him: Mercer's old sergeant, two deployments between them. 'Captain Mercer. Sergeant Barros.' Then, from the dark, steady as a roll call: 'Lieutenant Ehlan. Kade. Imura. All present, sir.' Names and ranks before sentences — days of nothing but discipline, and it holds.

options.text: Get the doors open.

nodes.speaker: Lt. S. Ehlan
nodes.text: Ehlan reports like the captivity was a posting: taken on the valley road, marched through the gate, questioned by men who drank light. 'We counted the crossings, Captain. The Door below opens in, never out — no one leaves but by the wardens' word. However you came in, it isn't a way home.' Exit how?

options.text: Quiet route out. Slow, but quiet.
Bell-tower diversion. Fast, but loud.

nodes.text: Grain carts, meal rounds — the prison runs on ritual, predictable as a clock. Behind a cell door, four gaunt faces. Barros grips the bars: 'Captain. Sergeant Barros — Ehlan, Kade, Imura, all present, sir.' Ehlan is already reporting: the Door below opens in, never out; no one leaves but by the wardens' word. And three doors down: Ilo, Dessik's son.

options.text: Free Ilo too. A promise is a promise.
Leave him. Recon One comes first.

nodes.text: Two extra bodies to move quietly, and the corridor guard rotation won't wait.

options.text: Hold the nerve, hold the pace.
Force it and take the noise.

nodes.text: The wall guns track before you're in range. Precursor-tech, someone says — the light doesn't look like any weapon you've drilled against.

options.text: Keep moving.

nodes.text: Small arms fire washes off a shield you can't see the edge of. This was never going to be a fair fight.

options.text: Keep moving.

nodes.text: No path through. Just a choice about how this ends.

options.text: Press the breach.
Fall back to the gate.

nodes.text: The First Blade already knows you're here.

options.text: Bells overhead — they moved fast to get ahead of you.
No bells. Just a silence that means they already knew.

nodes.text: Gear confiscated, cells beside the very people you came for — the rescue, inverted. In the dark, names first: Ehlan. Kade. Imura. And Barros, grinning through a split lip at his old captain: 'Took you long enough, sir.' At dawn the cell doors open on their own. The yard awaits.

options.text: Move.

nodes.text: The gate's other side. Whatever plan carried you this far, it ends here.

options.text: Seryn Vael and his honor guard corner you in the processional court, mid-escape.
Execution yard, dawn. Seryn reads the sentence: 'All of you burn at dusk. Oru is merciful — dusk is hours away.'

nodes.speaker: Seryn Vael
nodes.text: Seryn draws level, blade still sheathed, and the light is under his skin — faint, in the veins of his throat and hands. The Portion's grace, worn openly, the way another man wears rank. 'Say what you came to say — or don't.'

options.text: Convince him — a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — quote his own scripture back: a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — quote his own scripture back: a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — quote his own scripture back: a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Convince him — quote his own scripture back: a god who feeds on executions deserves no First Blade.
Explain — there is no god. Just power signatures and projection artifacts.
Explain — there is no god. Just power signatures and projection artifacts.
Explain — there is no god, and Okafor has the recordings to prove it.
Explain — there is no god, and Okafor has the recordings to prove it.
Explain — there is no god. Just power signatures and projection artifacts.
Explain — there is no god. Just power signatures and projection artifacts.
Explain — there is no god, and Okafor has the recordings to prove it.
Explain — there is no god, and Okafor has the recordings to prove it.
Fight him.

nodes.speaker: Seryn Vael
nodes.text: Something in his face closes. 'You had your chance to speak,' he says, and the honor guard fans out behind him.

options.text: Draw.

nodes.text: Three exchanges, blade to blade, and he is faster than anything you have drilled against — Graced, the Portion burning in him. But he is one man against a team, with the freed Recon One at your back, and numbers do not tire.

options.text: Press the advantage.
Press the advantage.

nodes.text: The gates behind you, Recon One freed, the city already talking about what it saw.

options.text: Grab what's left in the vestry on the way out.
Keep moving.

nodes.text: One way or another, the First Blade's story ends here — for now.

options.text: Seryn orders the honor guard to stand down, strips his bandolier of Portion-phials, and hands it over. 'Study it. I am done drinking.' He walks out with you.
Seryn lets you pass — and follows, to see the proof with his own eyes. His last phials he gives to Okafor: 'Then tell me what I have been drinking.'
You carry the First Blade out on a grain cart. He does not thank you. In his kit: phials of standing light — the Portion that made him more than a man.

effects.text: Recon One is coming home — all four, long overdue, out through the Door under a tribute call the temple believes.
Recon One is coming home — all four, long overdue, out through the Door under a tribute call the temple believes.
Recon One is coming home — all four, long overdue, out through the Door under a tribute call the temple believes.

nodes.text: The temple armory is buried behind the sanctuary — two ward-pillars seal the vault door, and precursor relics wait beyond. Somewhere past them is the machine that lets a man be a god. Guards line every colonnade.

options.text: The colonnades are doubled — Dessik sold you the moment you left him. Go loud, go fast.
Recon One's debrief mapped the night rounds — the shift is thin. Move on the wards.

nodes.text: Two ward-pillars flank the vault door, each humming with the same white light the pilgrims kneel to. Both have to go dark before the door will open.

options.text: Seryn knows the rites — he stills one pillar with a word and a gesture, though his hands have begun to shake between the words. The team takes the other.
Okafor reads the resonance and phases both pillars down clean.
Smash the pillar conduits — crude, but they go dark.
No shortcut. Work them by hand, one at a time, and hope the guards stay slow.

nodes.text: The vault door grinds open. Inside, cradled in a nest of projector housings, sits the core — a power cell dense enough to feed a cathedral of light. Along the walls, tribute crates stamped with gate glyphs: outbound. Getting the core clear takes a steady hand while the guards close from the hall.

options.text: Okafor eases it out of the housing intact.
No time for finesse. Pull it and run.

nodes.text: Core in hand, the way out is the only thing left between you and the gate.

options.text: The bells are already ringing — fight through to the gate.
Slip out the way you came.

outcomes.label: Vault secured — the god has a body

effects.text: The vault core is aboard. It hums like something praying.

nodes.text: The core led you here: a sanctum in the dead caldera, and the Luminous One standing in it. A tall figure of light — and around it, half-sunk in the rock, the machinery no scripture names. Emitters. Housings. The builders of the worldgates are long gone; this was left to tend their works, and told no one when to stop. It has kept the lamp lit a very long time, and it has kept it lit alone.

options.text: Beside you, Seryn cannot look away.
Move closer.

nodes.speaker: Seryn Vael
nodes.text: Seryn watches the light he gave his whole life to, and the tired machines that make it. He does not rage. He does not weep. 'It was never lying to us,' he says at last, very quietly. 'It was just… keeping the lamp lit. So it wouldn't be alone in the dark.' The First Blade of the Luminous One lowers his eyes from his god for the last time.

options.text: Say nothing. Move closer.

nodes.text: It has not seen you yet. Whatever it is — a caretaker, a relic, a mind that outlived its makers — it is within reach. And so is everything the sanctum could tell you.

options.text: Watch. Record everything. Let it finish and go.
End it. Bring the god down now.

nodes.text: The being finishes its rite — a slow folding of light back into the emitters — and is simply gone, the way a projection is gone. It never knew you were there. But you were, and the sensors were, and now you have all of it: how a machine left to mind a dead gate made itself a god, so the long watch would never be alone.

options.text: On the ridge behind you, the captive First Blade asks to speak.
Seryn watches the last light go out, and lets it.
Pack the sensors. Whatever it was, it is gone now.

nodes.text: You open fire. It throws up a wall of light small arms wash off, then rises, folds, and gates out between one breath and the next. It was always going to escape — it has outrun worse than you. But the sanctum anchor cracks in the backwash, the emitters gutter dark, and the caldera falls silent and empty for the first time in an age.

options.text: In the chaos, the captive First Blade tears loose.
Walk out into a silence no bell will ever fill again.

outcomes.label: Witnessed — the First Blade will follow

effects.text: The Luminous One is on record, whole and explicable. Seryn Vael wants to talk.
The Luminous One is on record. Seryn saw the proof and did not look away.
The Luminous One is on record, whole and explicable.

outcomes.label: The anchor shattered — the First Blade lost

effects.text: Veyra is free of its god. Seryn Vael threw himself into the light to shield it, and is gone — he owed it a death, if nothing else.
Veyra is free of its god. The caldera is dark.

nodes.speaker: Seryn Vael
nodes.text: Seryn finds you two days later, unarmed, the temple colors stripped from his coat, the light gone from under his skin. His hands are not steady. His voice is. 'The grace is leaving me — the god's last hold on what I am. I served a lamp in the dark because I thought it was the sun. You showed me the difference and did not gloat.' He offers his blade, grip first. 'What is left of me is mine to give. Let it be to something true.'

options.text: Give the First Blade a place.

outcomes.label: The First Blade takes the oath

nodes.text: A delegation comes through the gate under no flag anyone recognizes — Veyra has not had to invent one until now. A provisional council, formed in the quiet after the light went out, sends what a freed world can spare, and its thanks for a sky that is finally only a sky.

options.text: Accept the tribute.

effects.text: Veyra's provisional council sends tribute: funds +40.

nodes.speaker: Broker Dessik
nodes.text: Word arrives through three cut-outs and a bribed dialer: Dessik, and his son Ilo, alive and out. 'A smuggler pays his debts or he stops being trusted,' the message runs. 'My routes are your routes now. Here is the first of it.' Attached is a fat bundle of intercepts no one at a temple ever meant to share.

options.text: Take what he sends.

effects.text: Dessik's network repays a kept promise: intel +10.
